aliases: Grilled Mackerel Donburi with Miso Soup

english: Grilled Mackerel Donburi with Miso Soup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for mackerel donburi set meal; 'rice bowl dish' (FOODON_00002809) is the closest defensible parent class. The set meal composite nature (rice bowl + miso soup + drink) is not individually represented.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (fish and rice dish) is the closest defensible match for a grilled mackerel rice bowl. The full set meal composite (including miso soup and beverage) is not individually represented in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for mackerel donburi set meal. SNOMED CT does not routinely code composite restaurant meal sets.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal portion of approximately 600 g, comprising a mackerel rice bowl, a bowl of miso soup, and a choice of drink, as listed on the GrabFood menu.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Mackerel, Atlantic, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175136) and White Rice, cooked (FDC ID 169756) and Miso soup, prepared (FDC ID 172453); composite weighted estimate for set meal
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a composite mackerel donburi set meal. Nutrient values were estimated by weighting USDA FoodData Central entries for grilled Atlantic mackerel (~150 g), cooked Japanese short-grain rice (~300 g), and miso soup (~150 g) to a 600 g set meal total, then back-calculated to per-100 g. The input record's per-100 g values (energy 166.67 kcal, protein 8.33 g, fat 5.83 g, carbohydrate 18.33 g) are broadly consistent with this weighted estimate and have been retained with medium confidence. The beverage component is unspecified and assumed to be water or unsweetened green tea for nutrient calculation purposes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; white rice (cooked, Japanese short-grain) GI ~64 used as dominant carbohydrate component anchor
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a composite mackerel donburi set meal. The GI of 64 is derived from the dominant carbohydrate source — cooked Japanese short-grain white rice — as reported in Atkinson et al. (2021), International tables of GI and GL values, AJCN. Mackerel protein and fat content will moderately attenuate the effective GI of the meal. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (64 × 109.98) / 100 ≈ 22.9, where carbohydrate per 600 g serving = 18.33 × 6 = 109.98 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content of 291.67 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,750 mg per 600 g set meal), primarily from miso paste and soy sauce, approaching the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced miso paste and soy sauce quantities, choose unsweetened green tea as the beverage, and note that mackerel's omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) provide a beneficial counterbalance by improving endothelial function and reducing blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load of approximately 22.9 per serving driven by refined white rice (GI ~64) and low dietary fibre of only 0.83 g per 100 g (approximately 5 g per serving), which promotes rapid postprandial glucose absorption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should request a reduced rice portion or substitute with brown rice to lower the glycemic load, retain the mackerel portion for its beneficial protein and omega-3 content, choose unsweetened green tea as the beverage, and monitor postprandial blood glucose carefully given the moderate-to-high carbohydrate load of this meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Mackerel is a rich source of long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) which reduce triglycerides, improve endothelial function, and lower cardiovascular risk; saturated fat is low at 1.67 g per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This meal is broadly favourable for cardiovascular health due to the high omega-3 content of grilled mackerel; however, individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced soy sauce and miso to limit sodium intake, avoid alcoholic beverage options, and choose unsweetened green tea to maximise the cardioprotective benefit of this meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mackerel provides EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids that directly reduce joint inflammation per EULAR dietary guidelines; miso and nori seaweed provide additional anti-inflammatory and gut-health benefits.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should retain and maximise the mackerel portion for its anti-inflammatory omega-3 content, request reduced miso and soy sauce to minimise sodium-related tissue swelling, choose unsweetened green tea as the beverage, and avoid sweetened dessert or beverage options that contribute pro-inflammatory added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mackerel Don Set Meal (Japanese: サバ丼セット, romanized: Saba Don Setto; Chinese: 鲭鱼饭套餐; Malay: Set Makan Nasi Ikan Kembung) is a Japanese-style set meal served in Singapore, consisting of a donburi rice bowl topped with grilled Atlantic mackerel (saba) over seasoned Japanese short-grain rice, accompanied by a bowl of traditional miso soup (typically containing tofu, wakame seaweed, and dashi broth) and a choice of drink. The mackerel is grilled until the skin is lightly charred and the flesh is moist and flaky, then placed over warm, lightly seasoned rice. The overall flavour profile is savoury and umami-rich, with the natural oiliness of mackerel complementing the mild sweetness of the rice and the deep, fermented saltiness of the miso soup. Per 100 g, the set meal provides approximately 167 kcal, 8.3 g protein, 5.8 g fat (of which 1.7 g is saturated fat), and 18.3 g carbohydrate, with a sodium content of approximately 292 mg. The glycemic index is estimated at 64 (medium) based on the dominant white rice component, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 22.9 per 600 g serving. Mackerel is an excellent source of long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), making this meal broadly favourable for cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory health. The meal is moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium from miso and soy sauce, and moderate concern for type 2 diabetes due to the high glycemic load from refined white rice. This dish is a popular lunch and dinner option in Japanese restaurants and food delivery platforms in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a mackerel rice bowl set?
Is saba don good for diabetes?
Is mackerel don high in sodium?
Is mackerel don good for heart health?
Japanese mackerel rice bowl set meal nutrition
Is mackerel don suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a composite mackerel donburi set meal. Nutrient values were estimated by weighting USDA FoodData Central entries for grilled Atlantic mackerel, cooked Japanese short-grain rice, and miso soup. The input record's per-100 g values are broadly consistent with this weighted estimate and have been retained. The beverage component is unspecified, introducing additional uncertainty. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Composite meal estimation: No single approved-source entry exists for this set meal. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries (grilled Atlantic mackerel FDC 175136, cooked white rice FDC 169756, miso soup FDC 172453) weighted to a 600 g set meal and back-calculated to per-100 g. The beverage is assumed to be water or unsweetened green tea for calculation purposes. 2. Negative sentinel values: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient profiles, and moisture data for this specific composite set meal are not available in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as a single composite entry as of 2024. 3. Portions: The input record lists two portions with is_default: true for both, which is inconsistent; the 600 g single-person serving is used as the canonical default. The 1,200 g portion for two persons is noted but not used for GL calculation. 4. Input health_suitability data referenced mochi dessert and udon noodles, which are not described in the dish description; these references have been disregarded as likely copy-paste errors from a different dish. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; mackerel is permissible but miso and soy sauce may contain alcohol-based fermentation products depending on preparation. 6. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not constitute an approved nutrient source; USDA FoodData Central has been substituted as the approved source for nutrient values. 7. Multilingual local names added for Chinese (鲭鱼饭套餐 / 鯖魚飯套餐), Malay (Set Makan Nasi Ikan Kembung), Tamil (மக்கரல் டான் செட் உணவு), and Hindi (मैकेरल डॉन सेट मील) to satisfy validator requirements for a dish served in Singapore, in addition to the primary Japanese name (サバ丼セット).

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' — a valid value from the allowed list ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].

Iteration 3: Added mandatory local names for Chinese (chinese_simplified: 鲭鱼饭套餐, chinese_traditional: 鯖魚飯套餐), Malay (Set Makan Nasi Ikan Kembung), Tamil (மக்கரல் டான் செட் உணவு), and Hindi (मैकेरल डॉन सेट मील) to satisfy G6 and Q1 validator requirements for a dish served in Singapore.
Iteration 3: Added all new local-language names to aliases_by_language (chinese, malay, tamil, hindi keys) and to the flat aliases array to satisfy Q8 validator requirements.
Iteration 3: Verified aliases consistency — all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
